title: Lesson 8: Code Coverage and Execution Paths
description: Learn to understand how code executes by exploring different execution paths through a program.

sections.title: Understanding Code Coverage
sections.content:
Code coverage is a measure of how much of your code is executed during testing. In this lesson, you'll explore different execution paths through a program by providing inputs that trigger specific code paths.

This exercise will help you understand how conditionals work and how different inputs affect which lines of code are executed.

sections.title: Simple Branching Exercise
sections.content: Let's start with a simple example. The following function analyzes a number and prints different messages based on its value. Try to find inputs that will reach each part of the code.

coverageChallenges.hint: Try a negative number that's greater than -10.

coverageChallenges.expectedOutput:
Line 1: Number is negative
Line 2: Number is less than -10

Line 4: Number is positive
Line 6: Number is even
coverageChallenges.hint: Try a positive even number that's not greater than 10.

coverageChallenges.expectedOutput:
Line 4: Number is positive
Line 5: Number is greater than 10
Line 6: Number is even
coverageChallenges.hint: Try a positive even number greater than 10.

coverageChallenges.expectedOutput:
Line 4: Number is positive
Line 7: Number is odd
coverageChallenges.hint: Try a positive odd number that's not greater than 10.

coverageChallenges.expectedOutput:
Line 4: Number is positive
Line 5: Number is greater than 10
Line 7: Number is odd
coverageChallenges.hint: Try a positive odd number greater than 10.

sections.title: More Complex Branching
sections.content: Now let's try a more complex example with nested conditions. This function determines whether to approve a loan based on income, credit score, and employment status.

coverageChallenges.expectedOutput:
Line 1: Low income check
Line 2: Good credit despite low income
Line 3: Approved with conditions despite low income
Final decision: Approved
coverageChallenges.hint: Low income but good credit and employed.

coverageChallenges.expectedOutput:
Line 1: Low income check
Line 2: Good credit despite low income
Line 4: Rejected due to unemployment with low income
Final decision: Rejected
coverageChallenges.hint: Low income, good credit, but unemployed.

coverageChallenges.expectedOutput:
Line 1: Low income check
Line 5: Rejected due to low income and poor credit
Final decision: Rejected

Line 6: Sufficient income
Line 7: Poor credit despite good income
Line 8: Approved with conditions despite poor credit
Final decision: Approved
coverageChallenges.hint: Good income, poor credit, but employed.

coverageChallenges.expectedOutput:
Line 6: Sufficient income
Line 7: Poor credit despite good income
Line 9: Rejected due to unemployment and poor credit
Final decision: Rejected
coverageChallenges.hint: Good income, poor credit, and unemployed.

coverageChallenges.expectedOutput:
Line 6: Sufficient income
Line 10: Good credit and income
Line 11: Fully approved
Final decision: Approved

sections.title: Understanding Code Paths
sections.content:
Great job exploring different execution paths through code! Understanding how inputs affect which parts of code execute is a critical skill for programming.

Key takeaways:

1. Each condition in your code creates a branch point where execution can go in different directions.
2. When you have nested conditions, you must satisfy all parent conditions to reach the inner code blocks.
3. The same output can sometimes be produced by different inputs, but the execution paths may differ.
4. Testing edge cases (like zero, negative numbers, boundary values) is important to ensure all code paths work correctly.

In real-world programming, tools called "code coverage analyzers" help developers measure which parts of their code are being executed by tests. Aiming for high code coverage helps ensure that your program has been thoroughly tested under different conditions.
